observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed with the following courses of
action:

a) for the Inventory Committee to initiate the transfer of ownership of PPE
items that are already in possession of and controlled by end-users other
than the Municipality and comply with the necessary documentary
requirements. Alternatively, the LGU may execute a Deed of Usufruct to
formally authorize the concerned end-users to utilize the properties while
retaining ownership of the said properties; and

b) for the Municipal Accountant to:

i. effect the necessary adjusting entries to address the noted inconsistencies
in the depreciation computation of the LGU’s Property, Plant, and
Equipment (PPE), using the following pro-forma entry;

Account Title Debit Credit
Prior Period Adjustment xxx
Accumulated Depreciation-(PPE Account Title) xxx
To recognize the correct depreciation of Property, Plant, and Equipment
(PPE).

ii. conduct a comprehensive reconciliation of PPE records between eNGAS
and eCPAC. Review and validate all migrated balances to identify and
correct all the discrepancies in asset costs, accumulated depreciation, and
classifications;

iii. update depreciation schedules in line with COA guidelines. Ensure that
the straight-line method and a residual value of at least 5% are
consistently applied, as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2017-004;

iv. seek additional manpower or technical support, if necessary, to expedite
the reconciliation and correction process.;

v. implement regular reviews to ensure ongoing compliance. Establish a
periodic review process to maintain accurate PPE records and adherence
to IPSAS and COA-prescribed accounting policies;

vi. ensure derecognition of PPE items from the books of account of the
Municipality once the transfer of ownership of other PPE items has been
completed, in compliance with IPSAS 17. The net book value of the PPE
transferred shall be recorded as a debit to either Prior Period
Adjustment or Donations, whichever is applicable, based on the nature
and timing of the transaction; and

vii. ensure that the proper accounting and reporting procedures for all
acquisition/issuance/transfer/disposal of government assets are pursuant
to the existing manuals and other relevant laws, rules and regulations
after the completion of the one-time cleansing.

b) consistently observe the provisions of Section 62 and Section 64 of the
Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) Volume I
on the treatment of prepayments to achieve fair and reliable financial
reporting.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

a) the Municipal Accountant:

i. assign personnel to oversee the proper disposition of dormant accounts
and ensure adherence to the guidelines prescribed by COA;
ii. conduct a comprehensive review and validation of all receivable
accounts, with a particular focus on identifying those that have been
inactive for ten (10) years or more, and classify them in accordance with
the definitions under COA Circular No. 2023-008;
iii. initiate immediate actions for the disposition of dormant accounts,
including gathering and securing available supporting documents,
tracing debtors or accountable officers where possible, documenting
efforts to locate untraceable or deceased debtors, and recommending
qualified accounts for write-off, supported by complete documentation;
iv. formally follow up in writing with the Fraud Audit Office (FAO) at COA
Central Office regarding the pending issuance of the credit notice for the
liquidated Intelligence Fund account amounting to ₱8,480,000.00;
v. prepare and submit a formal request for write-off of eligible dormant
receivables to the COA, following the detailed procedures and
documentary requirements outlined in COA Circular Nos. 2023-008 and
2016-005;
vi. develop and implement an internal monitoring system to regularly track
the status and aging of receivable accounts. This should include the
assignment of focal personnel responsible for periodic reconciliation,
verification, and follow-up of outstanding accounts to prevent similar
issues in the future;
vii. implement routine validation procedures, including annual confirmation
of balances with debtors, documentation of collection attempts and,
immediate investigation of long-outstanding accounts to prevent the
accumulation of dormant and uncollectible receivable accounts;
viii. consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers with
outstanding dormant cash advances and are still employed with the
LGU, otherwise, further administrative actions may be pursued to
enforce compliance; and
ix. disclose all dormant or questionable accounts appropriately in the Notes
to Financial Statements in the absence of timely write-off, in order to
preserve transparency and provide accurate information to users of
financial reports.

b) the Municipal Mayor oversee the timely submission to COA of documentary
requirements for accounts recommended for write-off, including sworn
certifications, justification reports, and evidence of exhausted collection
efforts.

With the aforementioned deficiencies, we recommended the following courses of actions:
1. The Municipal Development Council, with the Municipal Mayor as
Chairperson, to ensure that all PPAs funded in the annual and supplemental
budgets are included in the approved investment program. The Municipal
Planning and Development Office, being the secretariat of the MDC, must be
cognizant of all new PPAs not previously included in the approved AIP and
consider its inclusion in the supplemental investment program before
endorsing the same to the local sanggunian for adoption and approval.
2. The implementing departments headed by the Municipal Mayor through the
MDRRM Officer, to follow the schedule of implementation of pre-disaster
PPAs as embodied in the approved DRRM Investment Plans. In coordination
with the implementing departments, evaluate the causes of delays in the
implementation of pre-disaster projects in order to provide immediate
resolution therein and lessen or eliminate the vulnerabilities of the community
in a timely manner.
3. The MDRRM Council through the MDRRM Officer, in coordination with
the Municipal Accounting Department, to properly identify the remaining
balances of DRRM Fund as a result of fully completed PPAs and consider its
re-appropriation to other priority DRRM PPAs which remained unfunded.
Also, evaluate the status of those unimplemented PPAs if the same is still to
be pursued, otherwise consider its realignment or reversion to other
necessary DRRM PPAs.

Therefore, we recommended that the Special Disbursing Officer, in his future undertakings relative to CIF, must adhere to item no. 6.1.5 of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG and DND Joint Circular No. 2015-001 dated January 8, 2015 by limiting the cash advance up to the 3-month requirement of the Municipality as regards to the CIF Physical and Financial Plan to ensure safekeeping of government funds.

Therefore, we recommended that the authorized person should submit the CIF cash advances voucher with its supporting documents as indicated in the transmittal letter within 7 days from release of the check and attach only the duly stamped “received” LR by the ICFAU for the previous cash advances made and not the LR of the currently released CIF check to facilitate the timely review of the documents by the audit team and fast tracking of the liquidation of the currently released cash advances for CI activities.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Peace and Order Council to scrutinize/examine thoroughly all proposals for Programs /Activities / Projects to be allocated under the Peace and Order Program budget before approval to ensure a safe, secure, peaceful and progressive community in the Municipality and to adhere to the definition of activities under item 3.23 of the joint circular.

Philhealth program out of the appropriation declared for the purpose.

Therefore, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the
Municipal Administrator to:
• Prepare a demand letter enforcing the decision of the Ombudsman to the
AO and mail the same to his last known address; and
• Ensure to immediately comply with the Ombudsman’s Compliance
Report, copy furnished the Office of the COA Audit Team Leader

Therefore, we recommended that the Special Disbursing Officer, in his future
undertakings relative to CIF, must adhere to item no. 6.1.5 of the COA-DBM-DILG-
GCG and DND Joint Circular No. 2015-001 dated January 8, 2015 by limiting the
cash advance up to the 3-month requirement of the Municipality as regards to the CIF
Physical and Financial Plan to ensure safekeeping of government funds.

In addition, to ensure that activities are carried as planned, cash advances
should be liquidated immediately as provided in item 6.2.2.1 of the same joint
circular and guarantee the release of the current cash advances on time thus the timely
delivery of activities.

Therefore, we recommended that the authorized person should submit the CIF
cash advances voucher with its supporting documents as indicated in the transmittal
letter within 7 days from release of the check and attach only the duly stamped
“received” LR by the ICFAU for the previous cash advances made and not the LR of
the currently released CIF check to facilitate the timely review of the documents by
the audit team and fast tracking of the liquidation of the currently released cash
advances for CI activities.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Peace and Order Council to
scrutinize/examine thoroughly all proposals for Programs /Activities / Projects to be
allocated under the Peace and Order Program budget before approval to ensure a safe,
secure, peaceful and progressive community in the Municipality and to adhere to the
definition of activities under item 3.23 of the joint circular.

Henceforth, we recommended the following:
a. For the MDRRMO, in coordination with the Council, to review and
update the LDRRMFIP annually
b. For the LDRRMC to prepare the LDRRMFIP strictly in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 by incorporating
the detailed plan for the unexpended balance of the 30% Quick Response
Fund (QRF) and 70% MOOE of the LDRRMF for proper utilization of the
LDRRMF.

We recommended that Management:
a. Allocate enough funds from the General Fund (GF) to subsidize the
maintenance and other operating expenses (MOOE) of the MDRRM
Office, in order not to lessen the fund intended for disaster related
activities.
b. Revisit the MDRRM Plan and make the necessary adjustments to ensure
alignment of PPAs with JMC No. 2014-1 of the NDRRMC, DILG, DBM
and CSC especially on the administrative expenses and honorarium aspect
c. Henceforth, stop granting honorarium to volunteers/responders in the
delivery of disaster risk reduction programs and activities. The local
government unit should provide them with compensatory benefits and
individual personnel accident insurance, in case of death accident, duly
approved by the Sanggunian.
d. Stop charging administrative expenses and other disallowable
expenditures against the Special Trust Fund (STF) and current year’s
MDRRM Fund.
e. Itemize all the projects/programs/activities that will be presented in the
MDRRM Plan.
f. Make representation with the Office of the Civil Defense to clarify issues
on the payment of honorarium to rescuers/volunteers.

Therefore, we recommended that the MDRRMO:
• To regularly review and update the LDRRMIF, in coordination with
the Council, to monitor the PPAs that have insufficiency of funds and
proper augmentation or supplemental budget shall be made
• To allocate enough amount based on the current or expected market
price considering the possible changes in prices in order to ensure
implementation of the PPAs within the time frame as planned.
• To utilize immediately the savings from the continuing
appropriations, upon proper resolution and update of LDRRMIP, to
augment the amount appropriated for rescue vehicle in order to reach
the amount equivalent to the current market price.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer to do the following to protect the government funds and the very liquid
asset of the Municipality:

a. Municipal Accountant:
Stop the practice of crediting Cash-Local Treasury automatically at
the end of the year to reflect with honesty, objectivity and consistency the
undeposited collection as of year-end. Present with accuracy and
reliability the correct amount of actual undeposited cash on hand as of a
particular period.

b. Municipal Treasurer:
Deposit collections made during the last working day of the year
and prior to the cut-off time of the depository bank to ensure minimal
amount left in the vault.

We recommended that the management correct its existing control in the
handling of cash collection and accountable forms by designating regularly
appointed or collecting officer in the collection of revenues and the custody and
issuance of official receipts in order to ensure accountability and responsibility on
government funds in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended to ensure the liquidation of all collections during the
day to the liquidating officer/Municipal Treasurer pursuant to Section 29 of
NGAS Volume 1 that is, to remit to the Liquidating Officer all collections made
during the day at the close of each business day for those situated near the
Municipality’s Office and at least once a week or as soon as the collections reach
P5,000.00 for those Collectors whose official station will take more than one day
to travel.

We recommended the following:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare an adjusting entry to arrive
at the correct balances of accumulated depreciation and net book values.
Below are the suggested journal entries:
DR Prior Period Adjustments P4,833,983.83
CR Accumulated Depreciation1 P4,833,983.83

b. Update the accounting system and conduct a simulation test to check if
the depreciations provided by the said system is accurate.

c. Conduct a random manual computation on the depreciation accounts prior
to financial statement preparation to check if the balances are similar with
the generated data.

1 Refer to Annex II-2 for amount of accumulated depreciation that need to be adjusted for each PPE
account. Note also that, working paper for the computation of accumulated depreciation and net book value
for individual PPE was emailed to the Municipal Accountant for reference.

We recommended that the Inventory and Inspection Report of
Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) be prepared and accomplished by the
Inspection, Appraisal and Disposal Committee. Proper and necessary disposal of
unserviceable properties shall be periodically undertaken in accordance with the
provisions of Section 79 of PD 1445. Further, the Municipal Accountant shall
adhere to the provisions of PPSAS No. 17 on the derecognition of the carrying
amount of an item of PPE.

The Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer shall
maintain the required PPELC and property cards. Reconciliation of the balances
between the RPCPPE and accounting records shall be required from the GSO and
Accounting Office. Any discrepancies between the records of two offices should
be reconciled and adjusted accordingly.

We recommended the following:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to
reclassify the advance payments to liability and asset accounts. Below is
the suggested entry for the General Fund and Special Education Fund,
GF Book Amount
DR Other Deferred Credits P 1,991,595.29
Discount on Advance Payment 500,014.40
CR Deferred Real Property Tax P 2,491,609.69

SEF Book Amount
DR Other Deferred Credits P 1,917,412.22
Discount on Advance Payment 479,353.05
CR Deferred Special Education P 2,396,765.27

b. Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile
the balances of the collections of property tax revenues.

We recommended the following:

a. Require the Municipal Engineer to facilitate the prompt submission of the
Certificates of Completion of the completed projects to the Municipal
Accountant’s Office;

b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to close the completed projects to the
appropriate asset accounts and compute the related depreciation expenses
accordingly;

c. Require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and Municipal
Planning and Development Coordinator to coordinate determine the status
of the projects with dormant balances and prepare necessary adjustments
after verification of the documents; and

d. Require the Municipal Accountant to determine the proper accounts of the
projects that are recorded as construction in progress and prepare the
corresponding adjusting entries.

We recommended the following:

a. Confirm the amount of the payables from the list of the identified creditors
to determine which of the payables are still valid and bonafide obligation;

b. Drop from the books of accounts the undocumented obligations /
payables;

c. Make necessary adjustments in the books of accounts if proven to be
without existing creditors for an accurate presentation of the payable
account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended the following:

a. Update and enable the accounting system in order to generate the separate
complete set of FS containing the necessary information such as the
balances of each accounts and financial condition and results of their
respective operations, in accordance with existing regulations for each of
the enterprise/special accounts; and

b. Require the Accounting Office to prepare and submit separate annual trial
balances and financial statements for the special accounts, together with
the FS for General Fund -Proper, Special Education Fund and Trust Fund.

We recommended that the management will review, scrutinize and plan
properly PPAs to be included in the AIP of the Municipality to ensure that all
approved PPAs will conform to the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-01.

Henceforth, it is advised that the Municipality will strictly adhere to the
provisions of the JMC especially on PPAs that are not allowed to be charged
against the 20% development fund.

We recommended that the management should itemize all projects
needing LGU’s counterpart to restrict that portion of the 20% development fund
pursuant to article 454 of IRR of RA 7160 and ensure continuous implementation
of the project without the need of any supplemental budget.

We recommended the following:



Strictly withhold all appropriations of continuing projects for
implementation in the following year.

Identify clearly all prioritized projects to be implemented in the current
year and conduct proper planning to ensure that what had been approved
will be implemented in the current year as scheduled.

We recommended the following:

a. Identify specifically the developmental projects to be funded under the
20% development plan before the approval of the AIP so that only specific
priority projects will be funded and to ensure measurement of performance
of the Municipality relative to the implementation of its developmental
projects;

b. Prepare the program of work and not only the project brief prior to
inclusion of the PPA in the AIP;

c. Apportion POW for the works to be done for a multi-year project to be
guided on what should be funded for the current developmental project. In
such a way, monitoring could be done if what is supposed to be finished
for the year had been implemented;

d. Stop the practice of forwarding the balances of completed PPAs which are
not continuing in nature, to the next year’s appropriation of similar
projects. If there are balances, declare the same as project saving which
could be used to other priority projects of the Municipality.

Require the Municipal Accountant to review the detailed schedule and analysis of Due from and Due to Other Funds and do the reconciliation accordingly.

Require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unobligated/ unutilized portion of current QRF and DRRM-MOOE to Trust Liability - DRRM account.
Likewise, disclose the utilization and balances of LDRRMF in the Notes to the Financial Statements.
Strictly adhere to the policies and procedures in the accounting and reporting of LDRRMF.
